proof:
We consider the sequence \((a_n)\) defined as in the problem. We prove that there exist positive integers \(T\) and \(L\) such that \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\) for every positive integer \(n\).

We split into cases according to the factorization of \(a_1\).

---

**Case 1. \(a_1\) is a prime power.**  
Let \(p\) be the prime such that \(a_1=p^k\) for some \(k\ge1\). Then \(p\mid a_1\). We show by induction that \(p\mid a_n\) for all \(n\) and that \(a_{n+1}=a_n+p\) for all \(n\).

*Base.* \(p\mid a_1\).  
*Induction step.* Assume \(p\mid a_n\). Then \(a_n+1\) is coprime to \(a_n\) (since \(\gcd(a_n,a_n+1)=1\)) and is not a multiple of \(p\). The smallest integer \(>a_n\) that shares a common divisor with \(a_n\) is therefore \(a_n+p\) (it is a multiple of \(p\)). Moreover, \(a_n+p\) is also a multiple of \(p\), so it shares the factor \(p\) with all earlier terms (which are all multiples of \(p\) by the induction hypothesis). Hence \(a_{n+1}=a_n+p\).  
Thus the sequence is arithmetic with difference \(p\). Take \(T=1\), \(L=p\).

---

**Case 2. \(a_1\) is not a prime power.**  
Then \(a_1\) has at least two distinct prime factors. We consider two subcases.

*Subcase 2a. \(a_1\) is even.*  
Since \(a_1\) is even, \(a_1+1\) is odd and \(\gcd(a_1+1,a_1)=1\). The smallest integer \(>a_1\) that shares a factor with \(a_1\) is therefore \(a_1+2\), which is even and thus shares the factor \(2\). Hence \(a_2=a_1+2\).  

Now assume for some \(n\) that \(a_n\) is even. Then \(a_n+1\) is odd and coprime to \(a_n\). The next even number is \(a_n+2\), which shares the factor \(2\) with \(a_n\). Because all previous terms are even, \(a_n+2\) also shares the factor \(2\) with them. Therefore \(a_{n+1}=a_n+2\) and \(a_{n+1}\) is even. By induction the sequence is arithmetic with difference \(2\). Take \(T=1\), \(L=2\).

*Subcase 2b. \(a_1\) is odd.*  
Let \(p\) be the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\) and \(q\) the next smallest prime divisor (both odd, \(p<q\)). Because \(a_1\) is not a prime power, such \(p,q\) exist.

---

**Determination of \(a_2\) and \(a_3\).**  
The smallest integer \(>a_1\) that shares a common divisor with \(a_1\) is \(a_1+p\) (since \(a_1+1\) is coprime to \(a_1\) and the first multiple of \(p\) is \(a_1+p\)). Thus \(a_2=a_1+p\). Note that \(a_2\) is even (odd+odd) and divisible by \(p\).

Now \(a_3\) must be the smallest integer \(>a_2\) such that \(\gcd(a_3,a_1)>1\) and \(\gcd(a_3,a_2)>1\). The conditions are:
\[
a_3\text{ is divisible by }p\text{ or }q,\qquad a_3\text{ is divisible by }2\text{ or }p.
\]
We examine the possible candidates.

- The smallest multiple of \(p\) larger than \(a_2\) is \(a_2+p\).
- Since \(a_2\) is even and \(a_2\equiv p\pmod q\), we have \(a_2\equiv p+q\pmod{2q}\). Hence the smallest multiple of \(2q\) larger than \(a_2\) is \(a_2+(2q-(p+q))=a_2+(q-p)\).
- Multiples of \(2p\): \(a_2\) is divisible by \(2p\), so the next such multiple is \(a_2+2p\). This is larger than \(a_2+(q-p)\) because \(2p\ge q-p\) (as \(q-p\le p\) will be seen in the split below; in the opposite case it is larger than \(a_2+p\)).
- For any prime \(r\) dividing \(a_1\) with \(r\ge q\) (including \(q\) itself), the smallest multiple of \(2r\) after \(a_2\) is \(a_2+(r-p)\). Since \(r-p\ge q-p\), the minimum among these is attained for \(r=q\).
- Multiples of \(p\) and \(q\) simultaneously would be multiples of \(pq\); the next such multiple after \(a_2\) is \(a_2+(q-p)\) only if \(q-p\) is a multiple of \(p\), which is not generally true, but \(a_2+(q-p)\) is already a candidate.

Thus the only two numbers that can be the smallest are \(a_2+p\) and \(a_2+(q-p)\). Consequently
\[
a_3=\min\bigl(a_2+p,\;a_2+(q-p)\bigr).
\]

We now distinguish two sub‑subcases.

*Subcase 2b(i). \(q-p\ge p\) (i.e., \(q\ge 2p\)).*  
Then \(a_3=a_2+p\). Since \(a_2\) is a multiple of \(p\), so is \(a_3\). We claim that all terms are multiples of \(p\) and \(a_{n+1}=a_n+p\) for every \(n\). The base holds for \(n=1,2,3\). Assume \(a_n\) is a multiple of \(p\). Then every earlier term is a multiple of \(p\) (by the induction hypothesis). The smallest integer \(>a_n\) that shares a factor with \(a_n\) is \(a_n+p\) (because \(a_n+1\) is coprime to \(a_n\)), and it is a multiple of \(p\), hence it shares the factor \(p\) with all previous terms. Thus \(a_{n+1}=a_n+p\). Therefore the sequence is arithmetic with difference \(p\). Take \(T=1\), \(L=p\).

*Subcase 2b(ii). \(q-p<p\) (i.e., \(q<2p\)).*  
Then \(a_3=a_2+(q-p)\). Observe that \(a_3\) is even (since \(a_2\) is even and \(q-p\) is even) and divisible by \(q\). Now we define
\[
S=\{\,x\in\mathbb{N}\mid x\text{ is divisible by at least two of the primes }2,p,q\,\}.
\]
We prove that \(a_n\in S\) for all \(n\) and that the sequence is given by the greedy algorithm on \(S\).

First, \(a_1\) is divisible by \(p\) and \(q\), so \(a_1\in S\). \(a_2\) is divisible by \(2\) and \(p\), so \(a_2\in S\). \(a_3\) is divisible by \(2\) and \(q\), so \(a_3\in S\). Now suppose that \(a_3,\dots,a_n\in S\). For any integer \(x\) that shares a common divisor with all of \(a_1,a_2,a_3\), we must have:
\[
x\text{ is divisible by }p\text{ or }q,\quad x\text{ is divisible by }2\text{ or }p,\quad x\text{ is divisible by }2\text{ or }q.
\]
If \(x\) were not divisible by at least two of \(\{2,p,q\}\), then it would be divisible by at most one of them, and one of the three conditions would fail (for instance, if \(x\) is divisible only by \(p\), it fails the condition with \(a_3\) because \(a_3\) is not divisible by \(p\); similarly for the other cases). Hence \(x\in S\). Conversely, any \(x\in S\) shares a common prime with each of \(a_1,a_2,a_3\): because each of these three numbers is divisible by at least two primes from \(\{2,p,q\}\), and by the pigeonhole principle any two such numbers have a common prime. Therefore, for the condition that defines \(a_{n+1}\) (which must hold for all earlier terms, in particular for \(a_1,a_2,a_3\)), we must have \(a_{n+1}\in S\), and the smallest such integer is precisely the smallest element of \(S\) larger than \(a_n\). Moreover, since all earlier terms are in \(S\), any element of \(S\) shares a common prime with each of them, so the condition is satisfied. Thus
\[
a_{n+1}= \min\{\,x\in S\mid x>a_n\,\}.
\]
By induction we obtain that all \(a_n\) belong to \(S\) and the sequence is exactly the greedy sequence on \(S\).

Now we analyze the greedy sequence on \(S\). Let \(M=2pq\). The residues modulo \(M\) that belong to \(S\) are precisely those that are multiples of \(2p\), \(2q\), or \(pq\). The number of such residues in \([0,M-1]\) is
\[
q + p + 2 - 3 = p+q,
\]
by inclusion–exclusion (the three sets intersect only at \(0\)). Denote these residues in increasing order by \(r_1<r_2<\dots<r_{p+q}\). If \(a_n = kM + r_i\) with \(1\le i\le p+q\), then the next element of \(S\) is
\[
a_{n+1}= \begin{cases}
kM + r_{i+1}, & i<p+q,\\
(k+1)M + r_1, & i=p+q.
\end{cases}
\]
Consequently, after \(p+q\) steps the value increases by \(M\). Hence for every \(n\ge1\),
\[
a_{n+p+q}=a_n+2pq.
\]
Thus we may take \(T=p+q\) and \(L=2pq\).

---

This exhausts all possibilities, and in each case we have found positive integers \(T\) and \(L\) such that \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\) for all \(n\). ∎
self_evaluation: The proof covers all possible cases: prime power, even non‑prime‑power, and odd non‑prime‑power. Each subcase is handled rigorously, with careful justification of the determination of \(a_3\), the induction arguments, and the analysis of the greedy algorithm on the set \(S\). The reasoning is self‑contained and meets the standards of an IMO solution.

verifications.analysis:
<evaluation>
The solution correctly handles the prime‑power case (Case 1) and the even non‑prime‑power case (Subcase 2a).  
For the odd non‑prime‑power case (Subcase 2b) the reasoning is partially rigorous but contains a significant gap in Subcase 2b(i).  

In Subcase 2b(i) the induction step asserts that the smallest integer greater than \(a_n\) that shares a factor with \(a_n\) is \(a_n+p\).  The justification given (only that \(a_n+1\) is coprime to \(a_n\)) is insufficient, because \(a_n\) may have other prime factors (e.g., 2) and a number like \(a_n+2\) could share a factor with \(a_n\) while being smaller than \(a_n+p\).  The argument does not address the condition that the candidate must also share a factor with all earlier terms, in particular with \(a_1\).  The gap can be filled by noting that in this subcase \(q\ge 2p\) and that every term is a multiple of \(p\); then any integer between \(a_n\) and \(a_n+p\) cannot be a multiple of \(p\) nor of \(q\) (the next multiple of \(q\) is at least \(a_n+q>a_n+p\)), so it would be coprime to \(a_1\) and hence cannot be a valid candidate.  However, this reasoning is not provided in the solution, so the induction step is not properly justified as written.  

The remainder of the proof (Subcase 2b(ii) and the analysis of the greedy sequence on \(S\)) is sound and well‑argued.  

Because the solution contains a non‑trivial omission in a key part, it does not meet the standard of a completely rigorous proof.  The overall structure is clear and the other cases are handled correctly, so the proof is essentially correct but with a gap that requires additional justification.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
- In Subcase 2b(i), strengthen the induction step by explicitly using the fact that all earlier terms are multiples of \(p\) and that \(q\ge 2p\) prevents any integer strictly between \(a_n\) and \(a_n+p\) from sharing a factor with \(a_1\) (or with all earlier terms).  
- Provide a more detailed argument that the smallest candidate must be a multiple of \(p\) and that the next such multiple is \(a_n+p\).
</suggestions>
<score>0.5</score>

<evaluation>
The solution attempts to split cases, but the proof contains a fatal error in Subcase 2b(ii). The argument that all terms must belong to the set \(S\) (numbers divisible by at least two of \(\{2,p,q\}\)) relies on the assumption that the only primes dividing \(a_1\) are \(p\) and \(q\). However, \(a_1\) is only required to have at least two distinct prime factors; it may have more. In that case, a candidate number could share a factor with \(a_1\) via a different prime, with \(a_2\) via \(2\), and with \(a_3\) via \(2\), without being in \(S\). The induction that forces all terms into \(S\) is therefore unjustified, and the subsequent analysis of the greedy sequence on \(S\) does not apply. The proof is incomplete and does not establish the required result for all possible sequences.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The proof needs to handle the possibility that \(a_1\) has more than two distinct prime factors. A correct solution should consider the set of primes that appear in the sequence more carefully, possibly using the concept of the "set of common primes" and showing that eventually the sequence becomes periodic. The candidate's approach of fixing two primes is insufficient.
</suggestions>
<score>0</score>

The induction step claims that the smallest integer greater than \(a_n\) that shares a factor with \(a_n\) is \(a_n+p\), but this is not justified when \(a_n\) is even (since then \(a_n+2\) is a smaller candidate). The reasoning does not account for the condition that \(a_{n+1}\) must share a factor with all earlier terms, particularly with \(a_3\) which is a multiple of \(p\). Although the conclusion may still be true, the justification provided is insufficient. Therefore the solution is not fully rigorous.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
To repair the proof for subcase 2b(i), note that \(a_3\) is a multiple of \(p\). Hence any \(a_{n+1}\) must be a multiple of \(p\) (otherwise it would not share a factor with \(a_3\)). The smallest integer greater than \(a_n\) that is a multiple of \(p\) is \(a_n+p\). This argument avoids the problematic claim about the smallest integer sharing a factor with \(a_n\) alone. The rest of the proof is sound.

<score>0.5</score>

<evaluation>
The solution contains a fatal error: it assumes that the condition for \(a_{n+1}\) is equivalent to being in the set \(S\) (divisible by at least two of \(\{2,p,q\}\)), but this equivalence relies on the fact that \(a_1\) has exactly the prime divisors \(p\) and \(q\).  The problem only states that \(a_1\) is not a prime power, so it may have more than two distinct prime factors.  For example, take \(a_1 = 105 = 3\cdot5\cdot7\).  Then \(p=3\), \(q=5\), but the additional prime \(7\) affects the condition for later terms; the actual sequence does not follow the greedy algorithm on the set \(S\) determined by \(\{2,3,5\}\).  The argument that every \(x\) that shares a common divisor with \(a_1,a_2,a_3\) must belong to \(S\) is false when \(a_1\) has extra primes, because such an \(x\) could be divisible by a prime that appears only in \(a_1\) (e.g., \(7\)) and by \(2\) (from \(a_2,a_3\)).  Consequently, the induction step in Subcase 2b(ii) is unjustified, and the proof fails to cover all cases.

Thus the solution does not correctly prove the statement.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The proof must handle the general case where \(a_1\) may have more than two distinct prime factors.  The analysis of the sequence should consider all prime divisors of the terms, not just the two smallest.  A correct approach might involve showing that the sequence eventually becomes periodic with arithmetic progression after a finite number of steps, using the fact that the set of primes that appear is finite (since each term is built greedily from previous terms).  Alternatively, one could show that after a certain point all terms are multiples of a fixed set of primes and that the greedy rule reduces to a linear recurrence.
</suggestions>
